data.title: Generac Soars On Another Earnings Beat, But Tariff Risks Remain
data.description:
Wednesday morning, Generac Holdings (GNRC) came in with their Q2 earnings, and while beating estimates was perhaps not that big of a surprise, the size of the beat impressed the market, resulting in the price of the stock soaring once again.

I initiated coverage on Generac back in late February, and rated it a strong buy because of the increasing demand for their products. That prediction bore out, but a lot quicker than I anticipated, and while the stock has done quite well since I first wrote about it, there is always the risk that the market is getting ahead of itself.

That’s going to be the goal of today’s reexamination, to both review the strong Q2 results and improved guidance, as well as to ask whether the market has totally priced the positive news in or if Generac remains reasonably priced given its performance.

Q2: Another Big Win For Generac

In April, Generac came in well ahead of estimates, beating by 29¢. Market reaction was tepid, and was tempered with concerns that the tariff situation is making overall economic progress uncertain. On Wednesday, they beat once again, coming in 33¢ ahead of non-GAAP estimates with EPS of $1.65.

Not only that, they offered more upbeat guidance for the rest of the year, pointing out that so far, their ability to raise prices without hurting demand has allowed them to more or less fully offset the costs of the tariffs in dollar terms. Whether they will be able to maintain this going forward remains to be seen, but they expect to be able to do so at least through the rest of 2025.

1H 2024

1H 2025

Net Sales

$1.89 billion

$2.00 billion

Gross Profit

$692 million

$789 million

Operating Income

$170 million

$195 million

Interest Expenses

($47 million)

($35 million)

Net Income

$85 million

$118 million

GAAP EPS

$1.38

$2.01

Non-GAAP EPS

$2.23

$2.91

(source: 8-K earnings release from SEC)

By the numbers, the thing to notice is that not only did net sales grow, but the gross margin actually improved from 36.7% to 39.4%. That didn’t necessarily translate to a huge improvement in GAAP operating margins, with the company adding to R&D costs as they try to grow into more and more market segments.

The conference call detailed their success, saying they exceeded even their own relatively lofty expectations. They knew there would be some growth, but residential demand was even higher than they expected, and favorable pricing left those gross margins better than they expected given the tariff problems.

Residential demand wasn’t the only strong suit, as Generac also saw strong demand for their backup products from data centers. This has led to a growing backlog of product orders that is likely to keep pricing strong through the rest of the year.

Demand is strong mostly in the US, particularly in the southeast where a bunch of recent hurricanes has a lot of people looking for backup solutions in case of weather events. Internationally, revenue growth has been more limited, with a much lower growth percentage.

What To Expect In The Second Half

Outlook was boosted after Q2 earnings. Though they expected the tariffs to drag margins down in their outlook presentation in Q1, so far that hasn’t proven to be the case. Demand has been strong enough for Generac that the company can effectively just raise prices to totally offset rising input prices from tariffs. The new outlook suggests they expect that to more or less continue for the rest of the year.

That could mean that the EPS estimates for the second half, $2.26 and $2.52 respectively, may not only be met but may continue to be exceeded.

Generac has had two big beats in the first two quarters of this year, and they had at least modest EPS beats in every quarter last year as well. Business is booming, and it seems the analyst estimates may still not fully appreciate the potential for growth.

If they even meet estimates for the rest of the year, the full year non-GAAP EPS would be $7.69, meaning a Forward P/E of roughly 23.40. That’s a bit higher than the sector median, but not dramatically so, particularly given how strong Generac’s business has been of late and how likely it is that they will continue to exceed estimates. From this perspective, at least, Generac does not appear excessively overvalued, but is trading at a premium I believe is likely deserved.

Not that everything remains a strength going forward. Generac expects they can keep raising prices with tariffs without eating into demand at least for the rest of this year, but whether that’s a long-term solution or not remains an open question. There are other reasons to doubt continued across the board growth.

Tariffs And Solar, Generac’s Risks

The tariffs are the most obvious risk posed to Generac going forward. Though demand for the product outpaces the harm raising prices has so far, if tariffs continue to rise and there aren’t a bunch of weather crises that inform the residential consumers of the need to buy at any price they can get the product, they could start to bite in the long run. Fortunately for Generac, though unfortunately for the people in hurricane-prone areas, climate change seems to be leading to more hurricanes and therefore more demand for generators.

The data center demand is also likely to continue to grow, for now. Data centers use a lot of power, and can’t afford to be down for long periods because of a storm. As the number of data centers increases, that too will be pushing them to buy more Generac backup solutions.

On the other hand, the higher prices because of the tariffs could drag down demand in other areas, and if retaliatory tariffs end up being put in place, those too could render Generac products less competitive in those countries. It hasn’t bitten Generac as much as was initially feared, but that doesn’t mean they don’t remain exposed to problems from the endless uncertainty of tariffs.

Another problem is the solar industry. While demand is growing in a lot of segments for Generac, the new One Big Beautiful Bill is dropping the renewable energy credits, and in the conference call Generac makes clear they are expecting a substantial contraction of the solar industry as a result.

You don’t think of solar a lot with Generac, but the company has actually been offering battery storage for solar solutions for some time now. The expectation is that this industry will be shrinking in the near-term, and subsequently Generac is trying to transition more of its effort toward greener pastures. Still, the loss of solar growth is liable to hurt, and how much it will impact the bottom line remains to be seen.

No Longer A Bargain But Still A Strong Choice

Back in February, I rated Generac a strong buy because it seemed very underpriced given their performance. That’s no longer the case, and the stock now trades at multiples that are roughly in line with what we’d expect from the sector. That said, Generac is a better performer than the rest of the sector, so if given the choice, I’d still prefer them over most others. That’s why I’m rating them a buy, though no longer so cheap as to be considered a strong buy.

Investors who own Generac should keep an eye on their ability to continue to pass along price increases to customers without hurting demand, because while they’ve been able to so far, in my mind that’s the clearest near-term threat, that customer willingness to absorb higher costs will eventually weaken. So long as people remain willing to buy Generac products at higher tariff prices, however, the company will likely continue to perform well.

data.title: MGIC Investment: Solid Q2 With Minimal Losses Persisting
data.description:
Shares of MGIC Investment Corp (MTG) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining 2%. After a strong run, the stock has pulled back over the past month as concerns have intensified around a slowing in the housing market. I last covered shares of MGIC in May, rating the stock a “hold” given my view the company’s genuinely strong fundamentals were already reflected in valuation. This has played out with the stock losing 2%, though given a 12% market rally, even a “sell” rating would have been justified. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit MTG.

In the company’s second quarter, MGIC earned $0.82 in adjusted EPS, which beat expectations by $0.10 even as revenue was a touch light at $302 million, as the company’s loss performance was better than I expected. Net premiums earned were essentially flat at $244 million. During the quarter, MGIC had $16.4 billion of new insurance written, up nearly $3 billion from a year ago. Its weighted average FICO score was still strong at 755.

Given the increase in origination activity, MGIC now has $297 billion of insurance in force, up about 1.8% from a year ago. However, revenue was flat because the portfolio yield ticked down slightly to 38.3bps. Since 2021, the mortgage insurance industry has been extremely profitable, earning returns well in excess of their cost of capital, as rising home prices reduce claims. This appears to now be eroding pricing slightly, which remains an item to monitor.

Persistency was flat sequentially at 84.7%. Because of elevated interest rates, refinancing transactions are relatively rare, and existing home sales are also running below historic norms. These two factors have combined to reduce loan turnover, leaving insured loans in place for longer. I view this as a positive as MGIC’s back book is extremely profitable, and the longer these policies stay in place, the better it will be for the company in my view.

While Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac will guarantee mortgages with up to an 80% loan-to-value, MTG and other private mortgage insurers help borrowers with low down-payments get a conforming mortgage by insuring the remaining balance. For instance, a homeowner may put 10% down and then have private mortgage insurance to qualify for a mortgage. Because mortgages are typically 30 years, these policies last for a long time.

As you can see below, MGIC’s portfolio composition is gradually changing but about 44% of policies are from 2021 and earlier. I view this back book as likely to experience virtually no losses. Home prices are up dramatically since 2021, and with every monthly payment, borrowers build up additional equity. While at origination MTG may only have had a 5-10% equity cushion, it now has a 20+% equity cushion underneath these policies, greatly reducing the probability of default.

By definition, more recent policies will be vulnerable to market downturns since they reflect the most recent housing market pricing. Here, we are seeing some softening, which has contributed to the relative underperformance of MTG shares lately.

We are seeing increasing divergence in home prices, and prices are now more clearly falling in the Sun Belt where there has been substantially more construction. Of course, with prices still up by more than 30% from pre-COVID levels, the level of home prices remains high. My view on MTG has been that its legacy book (2021 and earlier) is fairly insulated from home price movements and will suffer virtually no losses, given the amount of equity that has been built up. However, more recent vintages will face some losses, though to a manageable level. I view recent softening as consistent with the view that we have passed the peak in underwriting margins and that there will be some incremental losses.

I do believe that losses will still be low by historic standards. After all while certain markets (especially in Florida) are falling sharply, much of the country is still seeing some price appreciation. Importantly, Florida is only 6.8% of its exposure. Moreover, for MTG to face losses, it needs a borrower to miss payments and the house to be worth less than the outstanding mortgage. Even if the prevalence of negative equity is rising, unemployment remains low, which should allow borrowers to continue making existing mortgage payments. As such, I do expect delinquencies to remain muted, especially at MTG has maintained solid underwriting standards. For instance, just 26% of new loans have debt-to-income ratios above 45%, the lowest in over a year. Plus, only 7% of the portfolio has a FICO score below 680.

Indeed, credit quality is excellent. In Q2, MTG had just 2.2% of delinquencies, down 9bps from Q1 but up 12bps from a year ago. With solid prices and employment levels, I expect most of these to be resolved with no loss to MGIC. Indeed, in Q2, the company had $2.8 million of net recoveries. This was a primary driver of its earnings beat. With the portfolio composition changing, MTG will eventually again report losses, but that time is not quite yet. It now has $452 million of loan loss reserves, down $26 million from last year, and I continue to believe that reserves are near, if not at a bottom. While MTG is essentially a zero-loss business today, it is not prudent to assume it will be a zero-loss business forever.

Importantly, MTG is well capitalized to withstand losses. It has $2.4 billion of excess assets in the insurance company. MTG maintains a $6.1 billion investment portfolio. MGIC is earning just about 4% on its portfolio. In Q2, it earned $61 million of investment income, down slightly from last year. As it returns capital to investors, its investment portfolio’s size will face some downward pressure, offsetting the benefits of fixed-rate security reinvestment.

Given its strong capital position, MGIC made a $400 million dividend payment from the insurance operating company to Holdco. As a result, it now has $1.05 billion of holding company liquidity. These intercompany dividends are essential because the holding company does buybacks and pays dividends. In July, the company raised its dividend by 15% to $0.15, giving shares a 2.3% dividend yield. During Q2, MTG did $181 million of buybacks, and in July, MTG did another $68 million of buybacks. Its share count is down nearly 11% from last year.

Last year, MGIC paid $750 million in dividends to the holding company. With this H1 payment, MGIC is on track for a similar amount of capital transfer this year. Importantly, as policies continually mature, the operating company’s capital needs go down. As you can see, $450-500 million will be consistently released each year for several years. Of course, as MTG writes new policies, this creates new capital requirements. But with this ongoing rundown, MTG is well-positioned to dividend up all earnings to the parent.

Back in May, I saw MGIC’s run-rate earnings power at about $2.65 with $3.00 of 2025 earnings power. I see run-rate lower than current-year earnings because its loss performance is likely to be better than normal in 2025, and this will gradually normalize as its backbook shrinks. Ultimately, I see this reserve release-driven beat as more one-time in nature than a recurring item.

As a result, I now see current-year earnings of $3.00-$3.05. Share buybacks continue to come in strongly, reducing the share count and supporting slightly higher long-term EPS, but I still see about $2.65-$2.70 of long-term earnings from this business. With shares around $25, this leaves the stock at 9.4x run-rate earnings. I continue to view ~10x run-rate as fair and struggle to see much multiple expansion beyond that amid housing market concerns.

That does give shares about 7% of upside, or about 9% of total return opportunity, including the dividend. This remains below my buy threshold of 10%, but shares remain a “hold” given moderate upside. If increased housing market worries cause shares to dip below $23.50, I would be a buyer, as Q2 shows the MTG fundamental thesis is intact.

data.title: Intesa Sanpaolo Shatters H1'25 Earnings And Increases Shareholder Returns
data.description:
Investing in Banks

Who said banks are not a good investment? We all thought so after the Great Financial Crisis, and with some reason. However, it seems that as the landscape changed after the pandemic, banks are experiencing a true rebirth that attracts investors thanks to generous shareholder returns and impressive capital gains that we would have never imagined just a few years ago.

Even more interestingly, while North American banks seemed stronger, the European landscape was shunned. Among European banks, Italian banks were avoided like the plague, mainly due to country risk.

And now, guess who is beating the market by far? Two Italian banks that are becoming European champions: UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY, OTCPK:UNCFF) and Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY, OTCPK:IITSF). And, by the way, they are still trading at reasonable multiples, meaning that this growth has been fueled by fundamentals.

In this article, I will deal with Intesa Sanpaolo's latest Q2 report. With UniCredit, I have already done a review a few days ago, which you read here.

Right now, Intesa accounts for 15% of my largest portfolio. You guess right if you think that the majority of my position comes from gains. I picked Intesa as one of the key positions I wanted to have as we were coming out of the pandemic because I wanted to be exposed to the largest wealth management bank in Italy. As I often explained, I was particularly bullish on one trend. Italian household wealth is around EUR12.3T, with total deposits at EUR7.5T, of which EUR 5.7T is in financial assets. Moreover, Italians usually have low household debt and hold a lot of cash in their accounts (banking accounts increase by around EUR30B per month). This means that there is a lot of cash to manage. In addition, there is a wealth transfer while the population is—alas—shrinking. This concentrates wealth into fewer and younger hands that need financial advisory services. Therefore, I believe asset managers in Italy will benefit from several tailwinds.

The Macroeconomic Context

Not everything is rosy in Europe and Italy. Surely, the latest preliminary estimate of Italy's Q2 2025 GDP reads -0.1% versus Q1, when it increased 0.4%. The carry-over annual GDP growth is now 0.5%, which is miles away from what the U.S. is achieving. Core inflation is 2% YoY, with the service sector still putting price pressures on the consumer price index. While GDP is, in any case, increasing, business confidence is moving down with the uncertainty of tariffs looming on the horizon. We will see whether the US-EU trade deal will temper these fears and allow businessmen to regain confidence. The same is not true for the consumer. The confidence climate briefly plunged after Liberation Day in April but has quickly recovered.

Italian households have shown through many decades a propensity to save (58% of the adult population), both because of precaution and to target future investments or prepare for retirement needs. In Q1, the gross household saving rate was 9.3%, 60 bps higher than QoQ. While countries such as France can reach up to 18%, we see that, generally speaking, the average household balance sheet in Italy is strengthening.

The average CET1 ratio of the country's banking system is above 15%, slightly behind Germany and comparable to France. The whole banking system has gotten rid of the dangerous NPLs, with an average ratio below 3%. Currently, the ECB lending survey reported that Italian banks reported a 9% net increase in loan demand, while France witnessed a 27% contraction in loan demand. High capital buffers, low NPLs, and robust liquidity bolster the domestic financial resilience. This is why Italy's debt rating was recently upgraded to BBB+, which is still much lower compared to France's AA-. If Italy manages to keep its debt-to-GDP ratio from further explosion, thanks to faster GDP growth, I would consider another upgrade likely.

Intesa Sanpaolo's H1-2025 Earnings Review

Now back to Intesa Sanpaolo's results.

Intesa reported gross income up 2.7% YoY to EUR 7.9B, with an operating margin increase of 1.9%. Net fee and commission income rose 4.7% YoY, while net interest income was EUR 7.4B, down 6.8% YoY. Net income was EUR 5.2B (+9.4% YoY)—another six-month record for the third year in a row—and Intesa upgraded its FY outlook to more than EUR 9B. Why is this meaningful? Intesa targets to distribute 70% of its net income to the shareholders. This means that EUR 3.7B has already been accrued in the first six months and that at least EUR 2.7B is to be accrued in the second half of the year. This means that Intesa will have around EUR 6.4B for its dividends. With the contribution of buybacks, we could easily see Intesa return more than EUR 8B, which is equal to 7%. We then immediately understand why, in June, the bank launched a buyback program worth EUR 2B. The capital position was solid, with a CET1 ratio of 13.% while the NPL ratio was 1.2% net.

With interest rates moving south, it was not net interest income the main driver of this strong report. But we find that growth in commissions was truly important. But Intesa's cost management efforts are also paying off with annual savings worth millions (operating costs were reduced by 0.2% YoY, thanks to a decrease of 1.5% in personnel expenses and a reduction of 15.9% in SG&A expenses), which brings the cost/income ratio to only 38%, a remarkable metric. The annualized ROE is 20%. In the meantime, it manages over EUR 1.4T of customer financial assets, and its loans amount to EUR 419B. These figures show the banks' scale and capital solidity.

Intesa is nearing the completion of its 2022-2025 business plan. This means we will also soon see what its plans for the future are, and this will help us define reasonable expectations for the stock. In short, Intesa has managed to keep up its profitability, decoupling its profits from the interest rate cycle. As a result, its 2025 net interest income is expected to be well above the 2023 level. The transition to a revenue mix where net fee, commission income, and insurance income, with the addition of profits from trading, has been reached. Secondly, Intesa has been laser-focused on reducing costs and bringing almost to zero its NPL exposure.

Intesa Sanpaolo's Valuation

Intesa Sanpaolo trades at a PE of 11.2, which is in the mid-range of its 5-year average. The banks' P/B has expanded. At the end of 2024, it was around 1, while it is now 1.46x, reflecting stronger capital generation and investors' confidence in the bank's future. The dividend yield is just shy of 7% and, with the help of buybacks, could bring the total returns above 8%. When we look at Intesa's main peer, UniCredit, we see similar metrics. The PE is just a bit lower, while the P/B is almost the same. The dividend, however, is one percentage point lower, although UniCredit's commitment to buybacks is massive.

The current valuation reflects what I consider a balanced mix where investors are considering the strength of the balance sheet, the improved profitability, and the total yield they are receiving. Overall, Intesa Sanpaolo doesn't look like a screaming buy as it did until a few months ago. Yet, the stock isn't overvalued but appears fairly and rightly priced after years of improving fundamentals. There is still a lot of value to gain from being a shareholder of Intesa, and this is why I stick to my buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bitfarms Ltd.: Optimistic With The Buyback Plan And US Exposure Into 2026
data.description:
I have watched the evolution of cryptocurrency, particularly Bitcoin mining, which has evolved from a small-scale venture to an energy-intensive operation conducted on an industrial scale. Over the last five years, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has increased by 967.82%. Back in 2022, I wrote about MicroStrategy (MSTR) as a solid tracker of BTC, and it is up 703.95% (since then). Earlier on, I had focused on CleanSpark (CLSK) and its expanded mining activities, including sustainable mining, and the stock has since gained 19.09% (from 2021). In this article, I will explain why I am rating Bitfarms Ltd. (BITF) as a hold in light of the company’s buyback plan and its renewed focus on the US as a strategic location for growth. However, I will consider the growth in net losses ahead of Q2 2025 earnings that may hamper the stock’s rally into 2026, if it persists.

Bitfarms’ Strategic Realignments Into 2026

Bitfarms, a Canadian operator of data centers and a Bitcoin miner, is set to purchase 10% of its public float (of 499 million shares) in a share buyback plan beginning on July 28, 2025, and continuing through July 27, 2026. This 1-year program will see BITF retire about 49,943,031 common shares. As of July 29, 2025, BITF had a market capitalization of $710.32 million and an outstanding share balance of 557,548,857. At the current price of $1.24 (per share), BITF is likely to spend $62 million in the buyback plan. This change is likely to increase the share price past $1.30, and an increase in the market cap, as it will be seen in this article.

The acquisition of Stronghold by BITF in Q1 2025 also set the stage for the company’s expanded opportunities in the US (to about 80% of its North America energy portfolio, against 20% - international), which will prove pivotal in the future. It is all about the vertical integration and control of its power with the US exposure. In essence, a Bitcoin miner’s proximity to ample sustainable energy (in this AI race) is critical for revenue growth and cost optimization. Then again, the reduced rewards for blocks after BTC halving in 2024 also warrant BITF's growth into high-performance computing (HPC) and AI to create long-term value for shareholders.

Q1 2025 saw BITF record a 32.8% (YoY) revenue growth to $66.8 million against a net loss of $35.9 million vs. a net loss of $6 million in Q1 2024. Costs of revenues in Q1 2025 rose to an all-time quarterly high of $67.4 million. This increase is partly attributed to the acquisition of Stronghold Digital and asset write-down in Argentina. Revenues in Argentina declined 40% (YoY) to $6.955 million in Q1 2025 from $11.576 million in Q1 2024. The company also explained in its Q1 2025 management analysis that the supply of electricity to its Argentine facility in Rio Cuarto had been halted, affecting the intended long-term contract of up to 210 MW.

In regard to operational efficiency, about half of the company's revenue ($37.3 million) was proceeds from the sale of 428 BTC (from the total 693 BTC earned in the quarter) at an average price of $87,000.

Currently, BTC is selling at $118,140, indicating an increase of about 36% (from the last sale by BITF), slightly shy of its all-time high of +$120,000. Despite the high net losses, BITF has developed its culture of paying for its expenses using operating revenues that have been growing into 2026. Looking forward, BITF now has the energy infrastructure to reach the intended upside exposure coupled with rising BTC prices. By Q2 2025, BITF has about 1,166 BTC recorded in its balance sheet, and it has an opportunity to invest a significant amount of capital into its HPC and AI. Further, about 37.7% of its cost of revenues, or about $25.408 million, was attributed to energy expenses, which will be significantly reduced with the acquisition of Stronghold and the US expansion that is set to give a growth energy pipeline of nearly 1.1 GW in the US state of Pennsylvania (in the long run). As of May 2025, the total contracted energy capacity in BITF’s 15 BTC data centers (spread between North and South America) stood at 908 MW. Thus, the operationalization of the Stronghold energy pipeline will help to lower BITF’s costs into 2026 and improve the company’s profitability.

Valuation

I will use the net asset value per share metric to evaluate BITF since the company has a steady asset cover against liabilities. BITF has high net losses that undermine profitability, thereby making the price-to-earnings ratio ineffective. BITF’s net losses have increased 500% (YoY) in Q1 2025, while the adjusted EBITDA dropped 35% (YoY) to $15.1 million, down from $23.32 million in Q1 2024. I believe net asset value per share will provide a stable valuation, and I will then use this metric to determine the future price of the company.

As of Q1 2025, BITF’s asset balance stood at $777.003 million against liabilities valued at $112.294 million. Total equity into Q2 2025 is $664.709. It is noteworthy that BITF is expected to reduce its outstanding share balance by 10%, meaning that the outstanding share balance in 2026 (from the buyback plan discussed earlier) will be 507,605,826 shares. We will get the future price of $1.31 by dividing the total equity of $664.709 million by the expected outstanding share balance. This calculation shows that BITF's future price will be trading at a fair valuation of +5.65% (YoY). We have to keep in mind that the growth in BITF’s US activities will increase the asset balance by 2026, thereby leading to a subsequent increase in the total equity.

As seen in this net asset valuation analysis, only BITF is trading at a fair valuation with a potential upside into 2026. RZLV has a negative equity balance, meaning that it is insolvent (as far as its liabilities are concerned). MSTR and CLSK have a strong balance sheet as far as their asset values to liabilities are concerned. However, these companies have recorded significant dilution or an increase in the number of outstanding shares, that have led to lower forecasts. For instance, MSTR realized a 54.11% (YoY) growth in its outstanding share balance from 177.4 million shares as of March 2024 to 273.4 million by March 2025. CLSK realized a 23.26% (YoY) growth in its outstanding share balance in 2025, which has negatively impacted this forecast into 2026 despite the company's robust asset balance.

Risk

I have factored in the buyback plan announced by BITF in my valuation. Failure to actualize this share repurchase program will adversely affect BITF’s share performance, as elucidated in this article. I also expect the company to grow its asset cover into 2026 as it advances its US activities by stabilizing its energy infrastructure.

Bottom-Line

I have rated Bitfarms Holdings as a hold in this analysis with a focus on its buyback plan and continued expansion into the US. However, the company realized significant net losses into Q2 2025 that have dampened profitability. Still, my analysis shows the company is trading at a fair valuation with a potential upside into 2026. I will be looking forward to the release of its Q2 2025 earnings in August 2025 for the update.

data.title: Coca-Cola: Good Revenue Growth Prospects But Slowing Margin Expansion And Balanced Risk Reward
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Coca-Cola Company (KO) should see good growth moving forward. The company is seeing good price/mix growth, and its volume growth should also pick up in the back half as management ramps up growth investments (e.g., increased market spend in red and select markets).

Margin should also do well. However, the pace of margin expansion should slow compared to what we have seen in the first half, as the company steps up investments to drive growth.

The stock is trading at a small discount to its historical levels, but its valuation compared to its peer, PepsiCo (PEP), is at a good premium. While revenue growth is expected to accelerate, in part it is coming at the expense of margin expansion, which is slowing. The underlying execution is good, but I expect FX headwinds to continue. Also, I expect the risk-on mode to continue moving forward, making it difficult for a defensive stock like Coca-Cola to outperform.

Overall, I see a balanced risk-reward at current levels despite potential volume growth recovery and slightly lower-than-historical valuations. Therefore, I have a neutral rating on the stock.

Revenue Analysis And Outlook

Coca-Cola recently posted solid organic revenue growth of 5% in Q2 2025, driven by 6% price/mix growth that more than made up for a 1% dip in unit case volumes. About 5 points of the price/mix lift came from pricing actions, and another point from improved mix. The pricing increase in the highly inflationary markets contributed roughly 1 percentage point to total pricing, down from 5 percentage points contribution we saw for the full-year 2024.

Geographically, India and Mexico saw some weather-related weakness. However, North America and Europe showed sequential improvement. North America saw stronger volume trends and gained value share. All three EMEA segments - Europe, Eurasia & the Middle East, and Africa - posted volume growth. Latin America volumes were down, but the region still delivered organic revenue growth, helped by a stabilizing macro backdrop in Argentina. Asia Pacific was also mixed with volume declining, but revenue moving higher. China posted volume growth despite cautious consumer sentiment, while ASEAN markets like Thailand, Indonesia, and Vietnam remained soft.

Moving forward, I expect the company’s pricing and mix to continue helping the revenue story. The 6% growth in pricing/mix in Q2 shows management continues to implement pricing increases despite easing inflationary pressures. Till last year, the story was Coca-Cola increasing pricing in response to inflation. However, KO continuing to implement pricing increases despite fewer headwinds from inflation shows pricing discipline.

The company is making good use of revenue growth management tools, including AI-driven pack price channel optimization, which is now active in eight markets across four operating units after its initial success in Mexico. That should drive better pricing precision and faster market reactions over time.

On the volume side, I also expect some pickup moving forward. The company has done a good job in terms of pricing and productivity improvement, which is now showing up in margins/profitability. Management intends to invest a part of these gains in growth investments like increased marketing and new product launches, which should help volumes.

In Mexico, the company launched the “Juntos por Cien Años” campaign last quarter, highlighting its long-standing economic contribution while pulling forward World Cup activation by giving away 1000 tickets, resulting in a significantly improved monthly value share trend and consumer perception scores. In the U.S., the company saw successful innovations like Sprite+Tea in North America. These innovations have helped Sprite achieve the #3 position among sparkling soft drink brands in the U.S. I am expecting more such launches moving forward with the company focusing on growth investments.

The company’s Y/Y sales growth last quarter was also impacted by weather issues in India and Mexico, as well as geopolitical tensions. In India, monsoons arrived early, shortening the important summer selling season, while Mexico experienced uncharacteristically cold weather in June and was also hit by a major hurricane that slowed business. Further, the short-lived conflict between India and Pakistan also affected business performance. These factors are one-time in nature, and I don’t expect them to impact sales/volumes in 2H2025.

This, along with growth investment, should help the company return to Y/Y volume growth in the back half, which, combined with continued pricing/mix momentum, should help the company post good growth in the coming quarters.

Margin Analysis And Outlook

Coca-Cola posted strong margin gains in Q2 2025. Comparable gross margin was up about 80 basis points, while comparable operating margin jumped roughly 190 basis points.

The underlying gross margin benefited from price increases, which were partly offset by higher commodity costs. Operating margin saw an even bigger lift, helped by faster-than-expected productivity gains, which contributed roughly a third of the improvement. Cost control remained tight, and the timing of marketing spend worked in their favor.

The marketing transformation with more focus on media ROI also continues to show up in the numbers, driving better efficiency and impact. On top of that, tight management of operating expenses and well-timed investments helped support comparable margins. FX was a headwind for both gross and operating margins.

Looking forward, I expect the company’s margins to continue improving Y/Y. However, the pace of improvement should slow down a bit compared to what we have seen in the last quarter.

The company should continue to benefit from positive price/mix momentum. Coca-Cola has also transitioned recent bottling operations in South India to the Jubilant Group. This should shift revenue mix towards higher margin concentrate sales, which should also help margins.

However, there is going to be the same reinvestment pressure in the back half. Management plans to reinvest some of the first-half productivity gains into growth initiatives. We are likely to see a step-up investment in marketing and other initiatives, like new launches in the second half. So, the pace of Y/Y margin expansion should moderate.

Valuation And Rating

KO stock is trading at ~23.29x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $2.98, which is a slight discount to its historical 5-year forward P/E of ~24.15x. However, compared to its peer, Pepsi, which is trading at ~18x FY25 consensus EPS estimates, KO’s valuation is at a significant premium. Its valuation grade, according to Seeking Alpha quant rating, stands at a D.

While the company’s revenue growth outlook for the second half of 2025 is encouraging, it is coming at the expense of margin growth, which is slowing as management steps up investments to drive growth.

Also, while the company’s EPS outlook on a constant-currency basis is good (current-year expected EPS growth on a constant-currency basis at ~8%), adverse FX movement is a big headwind, wiping out ~5 percentage points from this growth.

While it is difficult to model currency movements, I see a risk that this currency headwind might continue moving forward. Trump targeting emerging economies like India (where KO has huge long-term growth potential) with heavy tariffs means the currencies of these countries should weaken for them to remain cost-competitive in exports. However, since they are fast-growth markets where Coca-Cola sells its products, weakening currencies in these countries would create a FX headwind for Coca-Cola’s sales and profit numbers when we look at those in terms of USD. So, even if Coca-Cola’s underlying execution is good, it might face slower earnings than its long-term growth algorithm.

Further, with all the talk about interest-rate reductions later this year or next year, there is a good possibility that investors remain in risk-on mode. I am not sure if Coca-Cola, which is a defensive stock, can outperform in the current environment.

Overall, I find the risk-reward balanced at the current levels and have a neutral rating on the stock.

data.title: Martin Currie Emerging Markets Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Emerging markets (EM) gained nearly 3% during the first quarter. The asset class saw a broad-based recovery across most countries, with only a few lagging in the period (namely countries in Southeast Asia and Taiwan). Key outperformers were China, led by strong returns from large-cap technology leaders. There were also positive returns from countries in Central and Eastern Europe following news surrounding moves towards a possible Russia-Ukraine ceasefire. During the quarter, India continued to see weakness, which was a result of foreign investor selling, muted earnings momentum, and domestic growth concerns. The small- and mid-cap space in the country underperformed due to further profit-taking.
  • Contributors: Single stocks that performed well during included Alibaba (BABA), Tencent and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF).
  • Detractors: Notable detractors included Globant (GLOB), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company and Quanta.
  • Outlook: We firmly believe that the long-term investment outlook for EM remains robust and we maintain strong confidence in our portfolio holdings.

Performance Review

  • Single stocks that performed well during included Alibaba, Tencent and BYD. Alibaba is starting to see very strong business traction in its cloud business and further positive developments in the company's leadership in artificial intelligence (AI). Broadly speaking, Chinese internet companies are strategically implementing AI in their business capital expenditures (CAPEX) and expectations are that AI capex could drive strong internet business models as well as promote a broad-based economic recovery. Tencent continues to deliver on its strong domestic growth engines (gaming and advertising) and has recently given more clarity on its AI ambitions and strategy. Tencent will utilize its own internal AI model and partner with third party models. The company's traction in AI to its consumer business has been recently growing. BYD maintained its leadership in both its domestic and its international electric vehicle (EV) businesses with strong monthly sales traction. The innovation pipeline in BYD is robust and the company is delivering EV vehicles with significant cost advantages, which is resulting in strong market share gains in overseas markets.
  • Notable detractors included Globant, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) and Quanta. Globant's 2025 outlook is lower than expected, with revenue growth being impacted by softer growth at its key client (which has decelerated from the previous year's strength). Furthermore, the company is seeing slower growth out of its Latin America business, which is reflected in its new guidance/outlook for 2025. TSMC has followed the weakness across most global semiconductor names during this period. Although key client Nvidia (NVDA) delivered a strong set of results, the market has been concerned about the potential impact of DeepSeek and the deflationary forces in play with AI hardware pricing. Semiconductor names have largely been impacted by slower consumer growth coming out of the U.S. market. Quanta was negatively impacted by shipment delays of GB200. However, the company continues to experience very strong market share gains in traditional servers, as well as strong potential from the GB300 rollout.

Outlook

  • We firmly believe that the long-term investment outlook for EM remains robust and we maintain strong confidence in our portfolio holdings. The market persistently undervalues high-quality, sustainable growth companies and we are convinced that despite the recent, prolonged style rotation, investing in these companies will yield positive results in the long term.
  • Looking ahead, we are particularly excited by the powerful synergy of technology adoption, urbanization, and services sector growth prevalent in EM. We anticipate that our highly selective, stock-focused strategy will thrive by seeking companies with high returns on equity, operating in structurally growing sectors. Furthermore, the current environment-with its regulatory pressures, human conflicts, and ever-evolving macroeconomic landscape- highlights the importance of focusing on strong sustainability and governance characteristics, and sustainable growth as critical factors in determining companies that will remain relevant and lead the way in the long run.
  • As we progress further into 2025, we think the key drivers of EM returns, and thus the biggest opportunities, will be technology, India, and China. Technology companies in EM are core to the global adoption of AI, and the longer-term structural growth opportunity of EM information technology is broader across the whole ecosystem. This should place them well to succeed in the next 12 months and beyond. Similarly, India has had an interesting 2024 and we think that as the market returns to recognizing fundamentals in share prices, there may be a market correction that benefits high-quality, sustainable growth companies. Finally, as the largest country in the asset class, China will continue to play a key role and we believe its recovery has only just begun.

Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights. Gross expenses are the fund's total annual operating expenses as of the fund's prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice. Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund's prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. The portfolio is non-diversified and may invest in a relatively small number of issuers, which may negatively impact the performance and result in greater fluctuation in value. The managers' environmental, social and governance (ESG) strategies may limit the types and number of investments available and, as a result, may forgo favorable market opportunities or underperform strategies that are not subject to such criteria. There is no guarantee that the strategy's ESG directives will be successful or will result in better performance. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Glossary

Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) are funds used by a company to acquire or upgrade physical assets such as property, industrial buildings or equipment.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund's portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure equity market performance in the global emerging markets. data.title: Expand Energy: Tax Savings Helps Offset Weaker Near-Term Natural Gas Price Expectations
data.description:
Expand Energy (NASDAQ:EXE) reported strong Q2 2025 results, with production around the high end of its guidance and operating costs and capex around the low end of its guidance.

Nearer-term (2H 2025 and 2026) natural gas strip prices have deteriorated a fair amount since I last looked at Expand. However, that negative impact on Expand's near-term free cash flow is largely offset by the positive improvements Expand has made in terms of capital and operational efficiency and the reduced cash income taxes as a result of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

Those items (efficiency and reduced cash income taxes) should also continue to benefit Expand in the longer-term, and thus I have increased its estimated value by $5 per share (from $115 to $120) in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment now.

I am thus modestly positive on Expand now that its share price has also declined by 7% since I last looked at it.

Q2 2025 Results

Expand's Q2 2025 results were positive, as it reported 7.202 Bcfe per day in average production. This was just above its 7.1 Bcfe to 7.2 Bcfe guidance range for the quarter. As well, Expand's capex for Q2 2025 came in at the low end of expectations, while its per unit operating costs were at the low end of its full-year guidance.

Expand's strong performance in terms of capital and operating efficiency has allowed it to trim its full-year guidance around operating costs and capex while maintaining its production guidance.

Tax Savings

As various oil and gas companies have recently noted, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act is expected to significantly reduce cash income taxes. This is due to the restoration of 100% bonus depreciation as well as the adjustment of the book minimum tax calculations for oil and gas companies (putting them on a level playing field now).

At $4.00 natural gas in 2025, Expand now expects around $125 million in cash income taxes during the year. This is a $200 million reduction from its previous expectations of $4.00 in natural gas. At $3.50 natural gas in 2025, the savings would be around $125 million (reducing cash income taxes from $200 million to around $75 million).

The positive impact is expected to be even larger in 2026, with cash income taxes potentially roughly $350 million lower than earlier expectations at $4.00 natural gas.

Other Cost Savings

Expand also has highlighted its cost improvements in other areas. It mentions a $125 million reduction in 2025 operating expenses and a $75 million reduction in its 2025 capex budget (while keeping its 2025 production target at around 7.1 Bcfe per day).

Thus at $3.50 NYMEX natural gas in 2025, it now expects around $1.725 billion in free cash flow, a $425 million improvement from its original projections of $1.3 billion in free cash flow at that natural gas price.

As well, Expand now expects $3.1 billion in 2026 free cash flow at $4.00 NYMEX natural gas (which is close to the current 2026 strip), up $500 million from its prior projection of $2.6 billion in 2026 free cash flow at that natural gas price. This is primarily driven by savings on cash income taxes but also by the progress with capex and operating expenses.

2H 2025 Outlook

Expand expects to average approximately 7.2 Bcfe in 2H 2025 production, which would bring its full-year average production to around 7.1 Bcfe per day.

The current 2H 2025 strip is around $68 WTI oil and $3.35 NYMEX natural gas. Expand is projected to generate $4.307 billion in 2H 2025 revenues after hedges at those commodity prices.

Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Natural Gas 1,213,296,000 $2.95 $3,579
NGLs 15,272,000 $26.00 $397
Oil 3,312,000 $57.00 $189
Hedge Value $142
Total $4,307

This leads to a projection of $802 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow for Expand, factoring in various cost reductions. This is lower than its 1H 2025 free cash flow, although including the impact of approximately $200 million in productive capacity investments that would help Expand reach its 2026 production targets.

$ Million
Production Expenses $350
Gathering, Processing, and Transportation $1,300
Production Taxes $100
G&A $100
Net Cash Interest $120
Capital Expenditures $1,535
Cash Taxes $0
Total $3,505

Notes on Valuation

I had previously estimated Expand's value at $115 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario. I have now increased Expand's estimated value to around $120 per share at those long-term commodity prices.

This increase reflects the boost in free cash flow (at long-term commodity prices) that Expand is expected to see from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act's impact on cash income taxes as well as Expand's improvements in operational and capital efficiency.

That impact on Expand's estimated value is partially offset by lowered near-term natural gas prices affecting Expand's 2H 2025 and 2026 free cash flow.

As an example, the 2026 natural gas strip price has gone down by around $0.40 since I last looked at Expand. That decrease in natural gas prices would reduce Expand's projected 2026 free cash flow by around nearly $1 billion before hedges.

As noted above though, Expand's free cash flow at $4.00 NYMEX natural gas is improved by $500 million by improved efficiency and reduced cash income taxes. Hedges and the further impact on cash income taxes from going from $4.40 to $4.00 natural gas also narrow the gap a bit.

Conclusion

Expand Energy has made improvements in operational and capital efficiency and also benefits from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. These items may boost its free cash flow by $425 million in 2025 at $3.50 NYMEX natural gas and around $500 million in 2026 at $4.00 NYMEX natural gas. However, near-term natural gas strip prices have declined, so my projections around Expand's 2H 2025 and 2026 free cash flow are now a bit lower than they were a few months ago.

Those efficiency items (and the effect of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act) should continue to benefit Expand beyond 2026 though, and I now estimate its value at around $120 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. This is a $5 increase from my previous estimate of its value.

data.title: Venture Capitalists Drive Q2 2025 Fintech Funding Above $10B With Mega Rounds
data.description:
Fintech funding rebounded sharply in the second quarter of 2025, reaching $11 billion globally across 390 funding rounds, according to our analysis of S&P Global Market Intelligence data. It was the first time fintech funding surpassed $10 billion since the start of the venture capital downturn in the third quarter of 2022. While deal volume continued to decline, investor appetite for high-quality opportunities grew, with average deal sizes hitting a two-year high of $28.2 million. Mega rounds surged to 23 deals, also the highest in three years, signaling renewed confidence in growth-to-mature-stage fintechs.

The take

The second quarter of 2025 funding data tells a story of capital concentration, sector rotation and investor discipline. It offers clear signals that fintech is entering a new phase — one defined more by quality than quantity. Investors are rotating out of risk-heavy models like lending and into capital-efficient, defensible verticals like payments infrastructure, data and insurance platforms. More money is going to North America, while other regions have yet to arrest the investor retreat. If US success stories continue to demonstrate profitability and scale, other regions could ride that momentum in the coming quarters.

Cross-border payments continued to attract capital, driven by ongoing demand for infrastructure that solves fragmentation in global money movement and investor interest in niche, tech-enabled corridors. At the early stage, the themes are more experimental but focused: Funding rounds led by startups specializing in agentic payments, QR-based fleet systems and bitcoin-native insurance show that infrastructure remains a fertile area for innovation.

Q2 2025 value rebounds but deal count slips

In the second quarter of 2025, global fintech funding reached $11 billion, marking a 22% increase from the first quarter of 2025 ($9 billion) and a 22% year-over-year rise from the second quarter of 2024 ($9 billion). This is the strongest quarterly performance since the third quarter of 2022 and the first time funding has crossed the $10 billion mark in nearly three years. However, deal volume continued to decline, with 390 deals recorded, down 13% from the second quarter of 2024 (450 deals) and flat compared with the first quarter of 2025 (393 deals) as investors remain selective and focus on high-quality startups.

There were 23 mega rounds (over $100 million) in the second quarter of 2025, up from 11 in the first quarter of 2025 and 15 in the second quarter of 2024. This 109% quarter-over-quarter and 53% year-over-year increase highlights the growing influence of large late-stage rounds in lifting overall funding levels.

Key funding rounds and emerging themes

Acrisure LLC raised a record $2.1 billion, making it the largest fintech funding round of the second quarter of 2025. The deal, led by Bain Capital (BCSF), lifted the US-based insurance broker's valuation to $32 billion, which marks a nearly 40% increase since its last institutional round in 2022. Acrisure is positioning itself as a tech-enabled financial services platform, with capital earmarked for strategic M&A and continued investment in AI, data, and analytics infrastructure. The firm's earlier acquisitions, including Tulco LLC's AI-powered insurance arm ($400 million) and QuickInsured.com LLC, underscore its technology-first strategy.

Cross-border payments represented a major funding theme, with over 10 deals globally. Singapore-based Airwallex (Singapore) Pte. Ltd. and Thunes each secured $150 million in late-stage rounds. Airwallex plans to deepen its global infrastructure, while Thunes aims to scale in the US, bolstered by recent regulatory approvals across 50 states. US-LatAm remittance startup Felix Technologies Inc. raised $75 million to expand its WhatsApp and stablecoin-powered money transfer network. Other noteworthy rounds include Fuse Financial Technologies Inc. ($6.6 million, Middle East and North Africa focus), OpenFX ($23 million for transparent FX rails), Payall Payment Systems Inc. ($1.5 million seed) and Pexx Technology Pte. Ltd. ($4.5 million seed for stablecoin-to-bank transfers).

AI is also reshaping the expense management fintech space, with Brazil's Clara Lending Co. and US-based Ramp Business Corp. raising capital to automate finance workflows. Ramp raised $200 million at a $16 billion valuation, noting that AI drives over half of its research and development output.

On the seed-to-early-stage frontier, the following funding rounds caught our attention:

– Meanwhile, a bitcoin-denominated life insurance startup ($40 million series A).

– Nekuda, building agentic payments infrastructure ($5 million seed).

– Piston Technologies Inc., QR-based systems for fleet payments ($6.1 million seed).

– P2P.me, enabling cryptocurrency QR code payments ($2 million seed).

North America surges, other regions pull back

In the second quarter of 2025, North America drove the recovery in global fintech funding activity, raising $8.0 billion, up 150% from the same period in 2024. The US alone accounted for $7.8 billion, making it the dominant destination for fintech-focused venture capital by a wide margin.

In contrast, Europe, the Middle East, and Africa experienced a sharp decline, with funding dropping by more than 50% year-over-year to $1.6 billion. The UK and the Netherlands were the region's leading markets, raising $513 million and $245 million, respectively.

Asia-Pacific remained relatively stable, bringing in $1.4 billion. India and Singapore remained the region's key bright spots, each attracting half a billion dollars in funding. Latin America, meanwhile, saw funding fall to $400 million, as VC activity in the region slowed amid macroeconomic uncertainty.

Overall, the second quarter of 2025 funding trends show capital consolidating in North America and select Asia-Pacific markets, while Europe and Latin America continue to face headwinds. Notably, renewed VC optimism in North America is driven by ongoing innovation and improving exit prospects as a number of fintechs listed (Circle Internet Group Inc. (CRCL), Chime Financial Inc. (CHYM), eToro Group Ltd. (ETOR) and Webull Corp. (BULL)) or are planning to list on US stock exchanges. Historically, North America serves as a bellwether for global fintech investment, so a sustained recovery in the US could eventually ripple into other regions.

Payments, insurance technology have a decent outing

Insurance technology led all segments with $2.9 billion, more than tripling year-over-year, as investors bet on digital underwriting, AI-driven risk assessment and embedded insurance models. Investment and capital markets technology followed closely with $2.5 billion, reflecting renewed interest in platforms serving institutional and retail traders.

Payments remained a stronghold, attracting $2.6 billion, up 37% from the second quarter of 2024, driven by demand for cross-border and real-time payment solutions. In contrast, digital banking and lending saw sharp pullbacks. Banking technology funding fell by over 50%, while digital lending plummeted 67%, indicating investor caution amid margin compression and rising credit risk.

Stage-wise analysis: VCs write larger checks, but pull back at early stage

Fintech funding in the second quarter of 2025 showed a strong return of investor confidence at the mature and growth stages, while activity at the seed and early stages revealed a shift in strategy toward caution and selectivity.

– Mature-stage funding surged 86% to $5.2 billion, with average round sizes rising modestly by 3.5%, suggesting a return of late-stage capital with valuation discipline.

– Growth-stage funding rose 11%, but deal count dropped 30%, pushing average round sizes up 59%, driven by larger, selective bets.

– Early-stage funding fell 17%, even as deal volume increased 35%, resulting in a 39% drop in average round size, reflecting cautious deployment. Seed-stage activity declined, but average round size rose 27%, showing capital concentration around high-quality early teams.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta: Path To A $1,000
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) reported better-than-expected earnings and revenues for its second fiscal quarter on Wednesday due to strength in the digital advertising market. Meta continued to perform and execute well in its core digital ad segment, seeing double-digit top line growth in ad-related revenues, and the social media company put up strong numbers in terms of free cash flow as well. Meta's guidance for Q3'25 was a blowout and indicates strong momentum in the company's core advertising business. In my opinion, Meta is a rock solid FCF and capital return play, and I believe it will only be a question of time until the social media company hits my new $1,000 price target as it accelerates its AI-driven growth strategy.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Meta a strong buy in my last coverage -- A No-Brainer Growth Play -- in which I highlighted the social media company’s enormous free cash flow power as well as consistency in growing its user base. In the second quarter, Meta continued to do very well in terms of free cash flows and grew its average revenue per user… which continues to underscore strong spending patterns in the digital advertising market. I believe it will only be a question of time until Meta's share price reaches $1,000, which is entirely possibly on the back of accelerating AI-related CapEx spending.

Meta Beats Q2’25 Estimates

The social media company reported better-than-expected earnings and revenues for Q2 and beat both measures by significant margins: Meta reported earnings of $7.14 per share, beating the Wall Street estimate by $1.24 per share. Revenue came in at $47.5B and exceeded the average estimate by a massive $2.7B.

In the second quarter, Meta generated $46.6B in total advertising revenue, showing 22% growth year-over-year amid a rock-solid digital marketing spending environment, characterized by lower inflation and strong economic growth. Meta's total revenues reached $47.5B in Q2'25, showing 22% growth as well. Importantly, Meta expects this strong revenue performance to continue in the third quarter, with the social media company projecting $47.5-50.5B in revenue, implying 21% Y/Y growth in Q3'25. The Wall Street consensus was only $46.1B.

Meta’s core business has considerable growth momentum right now, which is supported by the company’s strong position in the digital advertising market. In the second quarter, Meta generated 97% of all revenue from its core digital advertising business, while its Reality Labs division had a relatively minor 3% revenue share.

Meta continued to do well in terms of signing on new users to its various social media platforms (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp) in the second quarter. Meta had 3.48B users across its social media networks at the end of the June quarter, meaning the platform added a massive 50M new users throughout the second quarter. A growing user base is key to Meta's ad reach and a major lever for the company to grow its advertising impressions and revenues.

In terms of free cash flow, Meta is in excellent shape. In the second fiscal quarter, the social media company generated $8.5B in free cash flow, showing a 22% year-over-year decline rate. The drop-off in free cash flow, however, was driven by a doubling of Meta's CapEx spending, which is a theme I discussed at length in my last work on Nvidia (NVDA): Why I Am Aggressively Accumulating At All-Time High. To recap quickly, I believe that the AI/Cloud markets are set to see a massive acceleration of AI-related CapEx spending in the years ahead as AI promises unprecedented productivity gains across the industry landscape. Therefore, CapEx spending, although already surging, is very much in its early stages, leaving a long run-way for growth to be exploited.

CapEx spending, as it relates to data centers, could reach $1.0T by FY 2028, according to Nvidia, which represents a 4-fold increase over the spending levels seen in FY 2023. Since Meta is heavily investing in GPUs in order to run its large language models and in new projects dealing with Superintelligence, I believe the social media company is going to be at the forefront of this massive CapEx ramp.

Meta's CapEx spending has already exploded: the social media company spent $17.0B in total on capital expenditures (including payments on finance leases included in the table above), bringing the year-to-date total CapEx spend to $30.7B. According to the company's updated CapEx plan, Meta seeks to spend $66-72B this year on investment projects, which represents a $2.0B raise at the low end of the company's earlier guidance.

Catalysts For An Upside Revaluation

Meta has a number of catalysts in its business that could drive the share price higher. These catalysts include:

  • A strong corporate spending environment due to falling inflation and surging economic growth that supports ad spending growth. U.S. GDP grew 3.3% in the second quarter, crushing estimates. Strong GDP growth will be supportive of sustaining a favorable spending environment in the digital advertising market.
  • Strong core ad performance with rising prices: Meta's average ad prices were up 9% Y/Y in Q2'25, indicating that advertisers are willing to spend more on online ads
  • Meta's CapEx ramp taps into a growing total addressable market, driven by increasingly potent AI models
  • Meta's new AI strategy will focus on the creation of AI-supported 'personal superintelligence' which will help creators be more productive. This could lead to new revenue streams for the social media company going forward.

Path To A $1,000/Share

These catalysts could drive a major revaluation for Meta to achieve my new price target of $1,000 for shares of the social media company in the next twelve months.

In the last year or so, I aggressively recommended Meta, mainly for one reason: the social media company’s shares were massively undervalued in the big tech group consisting of Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta. The social media company outperformed all of its direct rivals in the big tech group in the last year, generating a return of more than 50% for investors, but the company's earnings potential is still quite cheap based off of forward FY 2026 earnings.

Currently, Alphabet is the cheapest big tech company with a forward P/E ratio of 18.6X, which is an absolutely irrationally-priced bargain, as I explained in my last work on the technology enterprise. Meta is trading at a FY 2026 earnings multiplier of 24.1X, which is below the industry group average P/E ratio of 26.0X.

Because Meta is seeing surging top line growth in its core advertising business, the company is likely going to see a major upside revision trend for its EPS estimates. Estimates for FY 2026 earnings currently stand at ~$29/share, implying only 5% Y/Y growth. In my opinion, the market underestimates Meta's AI-driven growth potential, and I expect the social media company to be able to earn $33-34/share next year, implying a more realistic ~20% Y/Y (at the midpoint) growth rate.

Applying a fair value P/E ratio of 30X -- which Meta could easily trade at given its core business momentum -- implies a fair value in the neighborhood of $990-1,020/share. This number is a number and could change based off of Meta's execution. If Meta were to see escalating AI-driven earnings growth, the social media company could ultimately end up significantly exceeding my fair value target.

Risks With Meta

Meta is growing rapidly, but as I indicated numerous times in my past coverage, the social media company also has the least diversified business model in the big tech group, and it is heavily reliant on just one core market: digital advertising. If the digital advertising market were to see deteriorating fundamentals, Meta's top-line performance would likely be severely affected. What would change my mind about Meta is if the company were to see significantly falling returns on its AI spending.

Final Thoughts

Meta is growing its business in a soaring digital ad market: its top line surged 22% Y/Y in Q2'25 as advertisers continue to spend big on online ads, and, as a result, average revenue per user surged 9% Y/Y as well. Meta is one of the most profitable companies in terms of free cash flow in the world and is escalating its AI-driven CapEx spending, which should lead to long-term free cash flow growth. In my opinion, Meta is, despite a strong stock performance in the last year, still very much undervalued, and I believe shares could be worth more than $1,000 in the next twelve months if the social media platform continues to improve user monetization, grow its user base, and tap into the surging AI market.
